responses.strategic_rationale: This residential parasomnia research unit navigates three fundamental tensions: (1) ecological validity versus operational safety—the domestic setting must capture naturalistic behavior yet protect vulnerable participants during nocturnal motor events; (2) data richness versus participant burden—intensive monitoring (video, EEG, sensors) yields clinical-grade phenotypes but risks suppressing spontaneous parasomnias if the environment feels too clinical; (3) event scarcity versus statistical power—infrequent episodes demand extended stays and large cohorts to achieve Aim 2 characterization, yet long admissions strain recruitment and budget. The selected levers target these core tensions by controlling facility design trade-offs, event-capture strategy, staffing scalability, participant retention mechanisms, data governance boundaries, and the event-triage algorithm's target use case. Together they determine whether the unit produces publishable longitudinal phenotypes or becomes underfunded and understaffed.

levers.name: Domestic Ambiance vs. Clinical Safety Architecture
levers.consequences: Immediate: The facility adopts minimalist safety modifications (padded edges, restricted windows, door alarms) integrated into residential finishes rather than institutional-grade cladding, preserving a private-home aesthetic that encourages spontaneous parasomnia occurrence. Systemic: Reduced environmental suppression of behavior is predicted to increase captured-event yield by 15–25% versus overtly clinical settings, but creates staffing complexity—technicians must interpret safety risks in real time without institutional safeguards, raising incident reporting and liability documentation by an estimated 20–30% workload. Strategic: This trade-off determines whether the unit generates ecologically valid phenotypic data or becomes a sterile monitoring box that fails to capture naturalistic episodes, directly impacting Aim 2 success and publication competitiveness.
options: Pursue full residential ambiance with discrete safety integration, accepting higher real-time risk assessment burden on night technicians and requiring enhanced incident-response protocols and insurance provisions.

Adopt a hybrid aesthetic using clinical-grade safety elements (grab bars, commercial-grade padding) in bedrooms and monitored areas while maintaining residential finishes in common spaces, balancing naturalism with staff liability reduction.

Implement maximum clinical safety architecture with transparent acrylic barriers, institutional flooring, and visible monitoring infrastructure, accepting 15–20% reduction in spontaneous episode capture as the cost of unambiguous safety compliance.
levers.review_lever: Controls ecological validity vs. institutional safety burden. Weakness: The options underspecify the actual occupant feedback loop—no mechanism is defined for participants to signal that an environment feels 'too clinical' or vice versa, so the chosen design may still suppress behavior despite intent.

levers.name: Event-Capture Yield and Stay Duration Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Participants begin with a 2–8 week baseline stay; those with zero captured events after the initial period are offered up to 2 extended weeks (capped at 10 weeks total), creating flexibility for sparse-episode participants but extending per-participant costs by an estimated €3,200–6,400 per extension week (staff, meals, facility overhead). Systemic: Allowing selective extension is projected to shift the unproductive-admission ceiling from the planned 20% to approximately 10–12%, capturing an additional 5–8 low-yield participants per cohort and generating 30–50 extra adjudicated events annually, but increases average stay length from 5 weeks to 6.5 weeks, reducing throughput from 30–35 to 24–28 annual admissions under fixed staffing. Strategic: This lever controls the Aim 2 event sample size and phenotypic diversity—higher yield at lower throughput produces richer longitudinal characterization but risks missing seasonal or trigger-based variation captured only through rapid participant rotation.
options: Enforce a fixed 2–4 week stay for all participants regardless of event capture, maximizing annual throughput (35–40 admissions) and reducing facility costs by €40K–60K annually, but accepting 25–30% unproductive admissions and weaker Aim 2 phenotypes.

Apply selective extension (current plan): offer 2 additional weeks to zero-event participants after the initial period, balancing yield improvement against throughput loss and requiring mid-stay re-consent and protocol amendment capacity.

Implement a tiered stay protocol where participants with ≥2 self-reported episodes per month begin with 1–3 weeks, those with 1 episode monthly start at 3 weeks, and those with <1 monthly begin at 4 weeks, targeting event capture predictively rather than reactively and reducing extensions by ~40% while maintaining higher yield.
levers.review_lever: Controls statistical power (sample size and events per person) vs. annual throughput and budget efficiency. Weakness: The tiered entry strategy assumes reliable self-reported episode frequency at baseline, yet parasomnia recall is notoriously poor; misclassification at enrollment could waste stays on participants unlikely to produce events.

levers.name: Night-Shift Staffing and Event-Response Coverage
levers.consequences: Immediate: The current plan deploys 3 full-time technicians rotating to cover 8 active suites (2.67 suites per technician during overnight hours), allowing 1 technician to attend to a flagged event while 2 remain available for monitoring and annotation, with a 30% surge buffer built in. Systemic: This ratio is reported to generate an estimated incident-response time of 2–3 minutes and false-alarm review burden of 8–12 hours per technician per week; if suite utilization reaches 10–12 beds, the ratio degrades to 3.3–4 suites per technician, increasing response time to 4–6 minutes and false-alarm review to 15–20 hours weekly, which approaches technician burnout thresholds and correlates with annotation error rates rising 8–12%. Strategic: Staffing scalability directly constrains facility expansion—the decision to expand to 12 suites by year two or remain at 8 units hinge on whether technician workload remains sustainable, affecting year-three revenue, Aim 2 event capture, and the unit's viability as a standalone operation.
options: Maintain 3-technician staffing and cap the facility at 8 active suites permanently, ensuring sustainable incident response and annotation quality but limiting annual admissions to 24–28 participants and raising per-participant operational cost by 18–25% versus scaled operations.

Recruit a fourth technician by month 12 and expand to 10–11 active suites in year two, maintaining a 2.5–2.75 suite-per-technician ratio, adding approximately €80K in annual salary and benefits but enabling 35–40 annual admissions and better cost-per-event recovery.

Implement a hybrid model combining full-time technicians (3 FTE) with cross-trained clinical psychology interns or postdoctoral researchers for supplemental evening coverage and annotation review, reducing per-unit technician cost by 20–30% but introducing variability in event-response expertise and annotation consistency.
levers.review_lever: Controls staffing scalability and operational sustainability vs. per-participant cost and event-capture yield. Weakness: The options do not address technician retention and burnout risk under high false-alarm loads—no mention of administrative support, annotation automation, or mental health resources for night-shift workers.

levers.name: Sensor Tiering and Data-Collection Burden
levers.consequences: Immediate: The plan employs a three-tier model—nightly low-burden monitoring (dry EEG headband, contact-free mattress, infrared camera, ambient sensors) plus scheduled enhanced nights (full PSG on nights 1, mid-stay, and final) plus escalation nights (full PSG after technician-flagged events). Systemic: This approach is projected to yield a physiological dataset with 95%+ temporal coverage (continuous movement, environmental data) paired with clinical-grade EEG on approximately 15–20% of recording hours (enhanced plus escalation nights), reducing per-participant PSG cost by 40–50% (estimated €600–800 per participant) versus nightly full PSG, yet generates a heterogeneous annotation burden—technicians must score low-resolution events on some nights and high-resolution events on others, introducing inter-night scoring variability of approximately 8–15%. Strategic: This tiering controls both the event-triage algorithm's training dataset composition and the Aim 2 phenotypic characterization depth; too much low-resolution data weakens the algorithm's sensitivity, while too much PSG exhausts budget and overstaffs the scoring pipeline.
options: Adopt nightly full PSG across all participants for maximum physiological resolution and algorithmic training power, enabling the most robust Aim 3 event-triage tool but consuming 60–70% of the 3-year equipment and staff budget on data acquisition, severely constraining Aim 2 sample size to 20–25 total participants.

Implement the tiered model as planned (low-burden nightly plus scheduled and escalation enhancements), accepting data heterogeneity and inter-night annotation variability while optimizing budget and sample size, requiring explicit sensitivity-specificity trade-offs in the Aim 3 algorithm.

Reverse the hierarchy: deploy nightly full PSG only for the first 2 weeks of each participant's stay, then transition to low-burden monitoring thereafter, capturing acute-entry effects and event clustering in the initial period with clinical-grade data while reducing costs in later weeks by 35–45%, but losing long-term within-person physiological stability information.
levers.review_lever: Controls physiological resolution and algorithm training quality vs. per-participant cost and sample-size feasibility. Weakness: The options assume the low-burden sensors (dry-electrode EEG, contact-free mattress) will generate sufficient signal quality for preliminary event detection, but this is unvalidated in a 2–8 week continuous-monitoring context; sensor drift or participant adaptation could degrade low-burden data quality by month 3 of operation.

levers.name: Participant Recruitment Filtering and Pre-Admission Validation
levers.consequences: Immediate: The protocol mandates a structured pre-admission pathway including specialist clinical interview, collateral history, prior/screening PSG, and exclusion-checklist adjudication by the PI, which reduces enrollment speed but gates inappropriate admissions (active substance use, untreated OSA, epilepsy mimics). Systemic: Strict pre-admission screening is predicted to reject 20–30% of self-referred candidates, extending recruitment time by 4–8 weeks per target cohort and lowering conversion from initial inquiry to signed consent by approximately 15–20% percentage points, but reducing in-facility safety incidents and false-parasomnia diagnoses by an estimated 25–35%, thereby improving Aim 2 data quality and publication credibility. Strategic: This lever controls both the recruitment timeline and the statistical homogeneity of the parasomnia phenotypes; loose screening accelerates enrollment but conflates distinct sleep disorders (e.g., confusional arousals misdiagnosed as sleepwalking), while tight screening delays the study but yields cleaner Aim 2 longitudinal characterization.
options: Adopt a simplified phone-screening model followed by in-person PSG only, reducing pre-admission time to 2–3 weeks and improving recruitment flow by 25%, but accepting 20–25% enrollment of non-parasomnia sleep disorders or medication-induced confusional states that later require protocol amendment or exclusion.

Maintain the current structured pre-admission pathway with PI adjudication, requiring 6–8 weeks from inquiry to enrollment, with dual external review for borderline cases to improve diagnostic confidence, slowing recruitment by 30–40% but achieving high diagnostic specificity and minimal in-facility protocol violations.

Implement a tiered recruitment model: fast-track clear-cut sleepwalking cases through abbreviated screening (3 weeks), while reserving full pre-admission workup for ambiguous cases (sleep terrors without clear motor components, rare phenotypes), maintaining 6–month recruitment target through case-mix stratification.
levers.review_lever: Controls diagnostic specificity and in-facility safety vs. recruitment velocity and enrollment rate. Weakness: The options overlook the reliance on collateral history from bed partners or household members, which may be unavailable for single-occupancy participants or those unwilling to disclose sleep behaviors; this could introduce silent misclassification of non-parasomnia nocturnal behaviors.

levers.name: Data Governance and Public Repository Participation
levers.consequences: Immediate: The protocol limits public data sharing to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements (DVAs), while bedroom video is retained privately due to severe privacy constraints and not deposited in public repositories. Systemic: This approach preserves participant privacy and reduces legal/regulatory overhead (estimated €40K–60K in additional GDPR and ethics review work if video were shared), but restricts academic impact and algorithmic transparency—external researchers cannot fully replicate event-triage scoring decisions without access to the video ground truth, potentially limiting downstream algorithm adoption and citation impact by an estimated 30–40% compared to fully open datasets. Strategic: This lever controls the replicability, transparency, and long-term influence of the Aim 3 event-triage tool; restrictive data governance protects participants but may prevent the algorithm from becoming a community resource or clinical standard.
options: Pursue full public data deposition including pixel-level de-identified or pixelated video (e.g., silhouettes only, geometric masks) alongside EEG and sensor streams, requiring enhanced consent processes, video anonymization validation, and 12–18 month ethics and legal review, but enabling full methodological transparency and maximum academic impact for the Aim 3 algorithm.

Maintain the current restricted-sharing model with DVA-protected physiological data only, preserving privacy with minimal overhead and allowing the algorithm to be shared under controlled licensing (e.g., academic research licenses, institutional collaborations), accepting reduced transparency and a narrower secondary-research footprint.

Adopt a hybrid time-locked release strategy: keep video private indefinitely, but deposit EEG, sensor, and manually adjudicated event-label data in a public repository after a 2-year embargo, allowing early users to develop and benchmark algorithms against the sensor and manual-score data, then release additional cohorts' data progressively, balancing privacy and impact.
levers.review_lever: Controls participant privacy protection vs. algorithmic transparency and community adoption. Weakness: The options do not specify how the de-identification validation of video (if released) will be audited for re-identification risk or how participants' rights to view or revoke their own data will be implemented under GDPR.

levers.name: Event-Triage Algorithm Endpoint and Clinical Utility
levers.consequences: Immediate: The protocol specifies that the Aim 3 event-triage tool will rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising independent diagnosis. Systemic: This conservative framing protects against overstatement of algorithmic capability (typical failure mode of early sleep informatics), but limits the tool's immediate clinical utility—the algorithm becomes a technician's 'filter' (e.g., 'flag top 5% of movement events for EEG-based scoring review') rather than a standalone diagnostic aid, reducing adoption friction in the facility but also reducing its commercial attractiveness or clinical translation likelihood by an estimated 40–50%, and capping publications in high-impact venues to methods-validation journals rather than clinical-application journals. Strategic: This lever determines whether the Aim 3 deliverable becomes a published methodological contribution or a stepping stone toward a phase-two intervention or diagnostic-validation study; the choice shapes the entire grant-funding narrative for year four.
options: Position the algorithm strictly as a review-prioritization tool, publishing Aim 3 results in sleep medicine methods journals with sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, explicitly disclaiming independent diagnostic capability and targeting adoption by research technicians within the facility and cognate research units.

Develop the algorithm with a dual-pathway design: a low-threshold 'event-candidate filter' for reducing technician annotation burden (published in methods venues) plus a higher-confidence subset of predictions paired with confidence intervals, proposing this higher-confidence pathway for future validation studies as a potential clinical-decision-support input (setting up phase-two funding).

Train the algorithm to estimate event-type probabilities (e.g., 'sleepwalking vs. confusional arousal vs. sleep terror' posterior distributions) and benchmark these probabilities against multimodal expert consensus (video + EEG + clinical history), framing Aim 3 as a phenotypic-stratification tool rather than a binary event detector, enabling preliminary claims about parasomnia subtyping that could attract pharmaceutical collaborators interested in drug-specific phenotype enrichment.
levers.review_lever: Controls algorithmic scope and clinical-translation risk vs. publication impact and pharma partnership attraction. Weakness: The options assume the dual human scorers will achieve sufficient inter-rater agreement (typically ICC >0.75) to serve as a ground-truth reference; if scoring agreement is poor (ICC 0.5–0.65), the algorithm's benchmark becomes contaminated and its clinical utility claim becomes self-referential.

responses.summary: The lever set is well-balanced across facility design, staffing scalability, data acquisition, recruitment, governance, and algorithmic scope—each addresses a core project tension. However, a critical missing dimension is *participant experience and retention beyond the study's explicit safety/medical scope*: the plan specifies compensation (€80 per night plus meals) and a 2–8 week residential stay, but does not address social isolation, sleep disruption from the monitoring process itself, or post-discharge follow-up engagement, which are known drivers of attrition in multi-week sleep studies. Add to Lever 2 (Event-Capture Yield and Stay Duration Strategy): 'Introduce a structured peer-support program pairing participants with overlapping stays, supplemented by weekly video calls with the clinical psychologist, to reduce mid-stay dropout and improve self-reported sleep quality; measure retention rate and mood stability monthly as a success metric alongside event capture.' This would strengthen both Aim 2 event yield (fewer dropouts = more complete recordings) and the facility's reputation for ethical, humane participant engagement.

responses.strategic_rationale: This residential parasomnia research unit navigates three core tensions: (1) ecological validity versus clinical control—naturalistic sleep behavior requires a home-like environment, yet parasomnia capture demands physiological fidelity and safety infrastructure; (2) operational scalability versus data quality—expanding from 8 to 12 suites increases participant throughput but strains technician bandwidth, event annotation consistency, and safety margins; (3) scientific independence versus sustainability—reliance on DFG funding ensures academic freedom but creates publication-timeline pressure and participant recruitment volatility. The seven levers below address participant selection tightness, longitudinal engagement durability, event-triage automation scope, the staffing model under surge conditions, facility footprint economics, inter-rater reliability governance, and external visibility. Each lever isolates a distinct decision that propagates across cost, quality, enrollment, and publication timeline.

levers.name: Pre-Admission Clinical Rigor Versus Recruitment Velocity
levers.consequences: Immediate: Stricter pre-admission screening (e.g., requiring two independent sleep clinic assessments plus actigraphy documentation) eliminates lower-confidence cases but lengthens recruitment cycles by an estimated 3–4 weeks per cohort. Systemic: Tighter inclusion reduces zero-event admissions by ~8–12 percentage points (from 20% to 8–10%), improving cost-per-adjudicated-event by approximately 18–22%, but extends year-one enrollment from 20 to 14–16 participants. Strategic: Higher diagnostic confidence at admission strengthens publication claims and reduces protocol deviations, but delays Aim 2 sample accumulation and risks missing the 50-participant target by month 24.
options: Conduct single in-person clinical assessment at University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic with structured questionnaire, prior polysomnography review, and bed-partner interview, accepting 18–22% unproductive admissions as baseline.

Require two independent specialist evaluations (sleep medicine and neurology) plus minimum 2 weeks of actigraphy baseline showing consistent parasomnia patterns, reducing unproductive admissions to 8–10% but delaying recruitment by 4–6 weeks.

Deploy a tiered rapid-access pathway: first-line telephonic screening by the study coordinator using standardized ICSD-3 symptom algorithm, conditional in-person assessment only for borderline cases, balancing speed with modest quality trade-off.
levers.review_lever: Controls recruitment speed vs. diagnostic confidence. Weakness: The options do not address how pre-admission delays interact with participant availability windows and seasonal variation in sleep disorder clinic referral patterns.

levers.name: Longitudinal Retention Engagement Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: Participants admitted for 2–8 week stays face monotony, separation anxiety, and motivation drift after week 3, risking early protocol withdrawal. Systemic: A structured engagement protocol (mid-stay psychologist check-ins, goal-setting interviews, simplified daily feedback on event detection) could reduce dropout by 5–8 percentage points (~3–5 early withdrawals prevented per cohort of 30), recovering approximately €12K–18K in participant compensation savings. Strategic: Lower attrition ensures minimum stay durations are reached, enabling longitudinal clustering analysis and reducing sample-size inflation needed to compensate for incomplete trajectories.
options: Deliver a single orientation session at admission and weekly optional group dinners with study staff, minimizing intervention burden but accepting baseline 12–15% withdrawal rates.

Implement twice-weekly structured psychologist check-ins covering sleep anxiety, home separation coping, and real-time event feedback, with a personalized weekly progress email from the PI summarizing detected episodes and phenotypic insights.

Introduce a participant research dashboard accessible via secure portal showing real-time event counts, comparative phenotyping metrics against their own baseline, and milestone celebrations (e.g., 'Event #5 captured'), gamifying engagement without introducing external reward systems.
levers.review_lever: Controls engagement intensity vs. technician and psychologist time allocation. Weakness: The options fail to distinguish between retention in early weeks (amenable to psychologist contact) versus late-stay engagement (where fatigue and diminishing-novelty factors may dominate irreversibly).

levers.name: Event-Triage Algorithm Automation Scope and Validation Pathway
levers.consequences: Immediate: Automating event-flagging (e.g., kinematic thresholds from mattress sensors + microphone vocalizations) reduces technician real-time annotation workload by 40–50% per night. Systemic: A semi-automated algorithm ranked at 85–90% sensitivity but 60–70% specificity (relative to dual human scorer consensus) generates candidate-event lists requiring technician review, yielding a net 25–30% reduction in total manual review hours versus purely manual screening, but introduces false-positive review overhead. Strategic: Moderate automation achieves Aim 3 deliverables (benchmarked triage tools) within computational scope, yet aggressive automation (>90% specificity) risks missing rare morphologies and delays algorithm development into year three.
options: Restrict automation to asynchronous overnight flagging: mattress + audio sensors trigger automated alerts, technicians confirm during real-time monitoring, two independent raters score only high-confidence flagged intervals, preserving human oversight but accepting lower flag specificity.

Deploy a semi-supervised ensemble (decision-tree classifier on kinematic + audio features trained on 10–15 manually labeled participant-weeks) ranked by confidence score, with technicians reviewing only top-ranked candidates during night monitoring and retrospective scoring on all flagged segments.

Develop a Bayesian hierarchical model incorporating participant-specific baseline motion patterns, circadian timing of parasomnia onset, and multi-modal sensor fusion, aiming for 88–92% sensitivity at 75–80% specificity and positioning the tool as a prospective clinical decision-aid in phase-two trials.
levers.review_lever: Controls computational ambition vs. manual-review sustainability. Weakness: The options underspecify how false positives (e.g., restless sleep, violent dream content) are distinguished from true parasomnia morphologies without subjective technician judgment, and do not address annotation drift when dual raters work across 18-month timescales.

levers.name: Night-Shift Staffing Elasticity and Event-Response Readiness
levers.consequences: Immediate: Fixed 3-technician rotation covering 8 suites (2.67 suites per technician per night) leaves minimal bandwidth for real-time event response, extended annotation, or sick-leave coverage. Systemic: Expanding to 4 technicians improves suite-to-staff ratio to 2.0:1, reducing response time by ~3–5 minutes per flagged event and enabling concurrent annotation of multiple events; estimated cost increase is €45K–55K per year (salary + benefits), raising personnel budgets by ~6–7%. Strategic: Better staffing elasticity improves event-capture quality and safety margins, but accelerates year-two budget commitment and constrains funding flexibility for computational infrastructure or algorithm development.
options: Maintain 3-technician roster with cross-training on event recognition and data-pipeline troubleshooting; deploy per-diem casual staff (registered polysomnographers) on call for event surges, accepting 8–15 minute event-response latency and bottleneck risk when multiple simultaneous flags occur.

Hire 4 full-time technicians structured as 2-on/2-off rotation, guaranteeing two independent observers per night shift and enabling real-time event documentation with <3 minute response latency; lock €50K incremental budget in year two.

Adopt a flexible model with 2 permanent full-time technicians (core coverage) plus 1–2 shift-variable technicians (10–20 hours/week) scheduled around predicted high-event-yield periods (e.g., participant weeks 2–4 post-adaptation), optimizing cost-per-event without sacrificing response speed on average.
levers.review_lever: Controls safety margins vs. operational expense. Weakness: The options do not model how event-response latency (minutes 0–15) affects physiological signal quality for retrospective scoring and whether faster response genuinely improves Aim 3 algorithm training or merely yields redundant video documentation.

levers.name: Facility Footprint Scaling and Lease Economics
levers.consequences: Immediate: The current lease secures 8 active suites with capacity for 4 additional suites (total 12) in the same property; expansion to 12 suites requires renovation investment (~€180K–220K) and increased utility costs (~€8K–12K/year). Systemic: Expanding to 12 suites by month 18 increases annual participant throughput capacity from ~35 to ~50 participants/year, reducing cost-per-participant-admitted by ~15–18% (amortized across fixed overhead), but only if recruitment channels sustain 40–50 admissions/year rather than the baseline 30–35. Strategic: Early expansion locks in lease-term favorable terms and demonstrates progress to funding boards, but risks stranded capacity if enrollment plateaus; deferral preserves optionality and conserves year-two capital but may require a second lease negotiation at higher market rates if expansion becomes urgent by month 24.
options: Renovate 4 additional suites immediately upon year-one participant stabilization (month 6–8), deploy to production by month 12, projecting 12-suite operations by mid-year-two and enabling 50+ annual admissions.

Defer physical expansion beyond the 8-suite baseline through year two; conduct a month-18 enrollment-rate review and trigger 4-suite renovation only if 25+ participants have been enrolled with consistent inter-admission intervals <2 weeks.

Negotiate a separate lease for a second smaller property (4 suites) in an adjacent Bonn neighborhood, distributing geographic footprint and reducing single-site operational risk, but incurring dual-site logistics and requiring two separate night-shift teams (~€35K additional annual cost).
levers.review_lever: Controls capital efficiency vs. operational flexibility. Weakness: The options ignore how dual-site operations (if chosen) affect inter-rater reliability training, centralized data-pipeline management, and the ability of a single PI to maintain oversight across dispersed facilities.

levers.name: Inter-Rater Reliability Governance and Annotation Protocol Consistency
levers.consequences: Immediate: Two independent raters score all flagged events using AASM criteria with discordance logged; unresolved disagreements (kappa <0.75 on subscale) trigger a third expert referee or protocol clarification. Systemic: Implementing quarterly calibration sessions (both raters score the same 5–10 reference events and discuss discordance patterns) maintains inter-rater reliability above κ=0.80 across the 18-month scoring window, estimated to cost €8K/year in senior sleep-medicine physician time. Strategic: Rigorous inter-rater governance strengthens publication credibility and Aim 3 algorithm benchmarking, but adds administrative overhead and risks publication delays if referee discordance is frequent enough to warrant protocol amendments.
options: Two independent raters score all events with discordance recorded; unresolved disagreements are marked as inconclusive, excluded from Aim 2 analysis, and reported as a sensitivity estimate in methods.

Conduct quarterly joint-scoring calibration sessions with 10 reference events per session; track kappa by rater-pair and event morphology; introduce a standing third-rater protocol if rolling 30-event kappa drops below 0.78, with consensus scoring and root-cause analysis (fatigue, protocol drift, ambiguous morphology).

Employ a single expert (senior sleep neurophysiologist at University Hospital Bonn, blinded to participant identity) as primary scorer, with secondary verification by the postdoc on 20% random sample; discordance triggers discussion and protocol refinement, reducing overhead but concentrating decision authority.
levers.review_lever: Controls annotation consistency vs. resource expenditure. Weakness: The options do not specify how seasonal changes in rater availability (e.g., clinical leave, grant deadlines) or the accumulation of borderline cases late in the project cycle affect scoring backlog and publication timelines.

levers.name: External Scientific Visibility and Publication Sequencing Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Delaying major publications until month 24 (after Aim 2 and Aim 3 completion) maximizes comprehensive findings but risks competitor pre-emption and funder perception of slow progress. Systemic: A staged publication model (methods validation at month 12, preliminary phenotyping at month 18, algorithm benchmarking at month 24) requires 3–4 concurrent manuscripts in preparation, increasing PI and postdoc writing burden by ~15–20% annually but distributing evidence-of-progress across two funding-report cycles. Strategic: Early partial publications accelerate scientific impact and bolster year-two funding renewals, but may commit preliminary findings to print before full dataset maturation, risking critiques of incomplete sample size or missed confounders.
options: Reserve all publications for the 24–36 month window; submit a single comprehensive methods + phenotyping + algorithm paper at month 28, prioritizing depth over visibility and avoiding preliminary-findings criticism.

Publish a methods-validation manuscript at month 12 (residential capture safety, data quality, inter-rater reliability), a longitudinal-characterization paper at month 20 (first 40–50 participants, within-subject clustering, phenotypic subgroups), and an algorithm-benchmarking paper at month 30.

Deploy rapid-turnaround preprints beginning at month 9 (preliminary safety data), followed by peer-reviewed methods paper at month 14 and a living-document preprint series tracking algorithm development in parallel with manuscript preparation, maximizing early-stage visibility and inviting community feedback.
levers.review_lever: Controls publication urgency vs. scientific maturity. Weakness: The options do not account for journal review timelines (3–6 months peer review + revision), which may decouple planned submission dates from actual publication dates and complicate funder-narrative alignment across years two and three.

responses.summary: The seven levers are well-differentiated and span recruitment tightness, retention, automation scope, staffing economics, facility scaling, annotation rigor, and visibility—addressing the unit's core operational and scientific trade-offs. However, a critical missing dimension is **inter-participant data-sharing governance and real-time feedback loops to participants themselves**. Currently, the plan deposits de-identified physiological data in public repositories but does not specify how or whether individual participants receive results, phenotypic insights, or actionable clinical feedback during their stay. Add to Lever 2 (Retention Engagement): 'Develop a secure participant-results portal that displays their individual event frequency, morphology classification, and comparison-to-cohort phenotypic patterns in real-time, enabling personalized feedback sessions with the psychologist mid-stay and improving motivation while generating a post-discharge participant handout with clinically contextualized findings.' This closes the feedback loop, aligns with emerging participant-centered research norms, and may strengthen retention without major cost penalty.

responses.strategic_rationale: This longitudinal parasomnia facility must navigate three fundamental tensions: (1) ecological validity (domestic ambiance enabling naturalistic behavior) versus detection sensitivity (clinical rigor capturing infrequent events); (2) operational sustainability (manageable technician burden, cost containment, steady enrollment) versus scientific yield (sufficient event capture and phenotypic diversity for publication); (3) team expertise deployment (sleep medicine depth versus computational/engineering breadth) versus financial constraint (€3.8M fixed, personnel-heavy). The selected levers address these core conflicts by forcing choices about facility homogeneity, data acquisition philosophy, team composition, publication strategy, and risk tolerance for participant safety—each lever reveals a hidden trade-off that will shape the project's success or failure over 36 months.

levers.name: Domestic Materiality Calibration and Behavioral Naturalism Risk
levers.consequences: Immediate: Choosing heavy residential décor and sound dampening versus minimal clinical fixtures directly alters how participants move, sleep position, and arouse during night monitoring. Systemic: Overly domestic settings reduce technician vigilance and event-detection latency by an estimated 15–25%, increasing missed-event risk and delaying clinical response, while overly clinical décor suppresses parasomnia expression by 20–35% based on environment-behavior literature in sleep disorders. Strategic: The tension between ecological validity and safety assurance determines whether the facility captures authentic parasomnia phenotypes or merely documents artifact-laden behavior in an unfamiliar setting, directly affecting Aim 2 longitudinal characterization and publication credibility.
options: Design the residential core (bedrooms, hallways, living areas) with full domestic furnishings, warm lighting, personal artwork, and family-grade soft goods, accepting that technicians must rotate through a formal safety checklist every 2 hours to maintain event-response readiness.

Implement a hybrid model with domestic sleeping quarters but a dedicated clinical observation and intervention zone (adjacent safety room with monitors, crash cart, panic buttons) where technicians remain stationed during high-risk hours (22:00–06:00), preserving bedroom authenticity while centralizing safety control.

Adopt a minimalist, safety-optimized interior with permanent clinical-grade fixtures (washable surfaces, fixed furniture, medical-standard alarm systems), relying on subtle acoustic and lighting design to approximate residential comfort while accepting a 15–20% reduction in event yield as the trade-off for maximum technician efficiency and incident prevention.
levers.review_lever: Controls environment authenticity vs. clinical safety optimization. Weakness: The options do not address how facility ambiance affects long-term participant retention or the cost of retrofitting if the pilot cohort reports psychological dissatisfaction with their assigned setting.

levers.name: Escalation Polysomnography Trigger Design and Event-Capture Sequencing
levers.consequences: Immediate: Defining when to deploy full clinical-grade PSG (scheduled enhanced nights on days 1, mid-stay, final day versus technician-flagged reactive escalation versus random adaptive scheduling) determines staffing burden and equipment utilization. Systemic: Scheduled-only PSG reduces equipment cost by ~€120K over 3 years and technician overtime by 30% but misses 40–50% of infrequent parasomnia episodes; reactive escalation increases capture sensitivity to 75–85% but requires 2 additional night technicians (€180K salary overhead) and unpredictable after-hours PSG setup costs of €60–90K annually. Strategic: This lever determines whether the project can fund Aim 3 (semi-automated event-triage benchmarking) with sufficient event-label density, or whether Aim 2 will be underpowered for temporal clustering analysis, directly affecting publication scope and grant renewal viability.
options: Reserve full PSG to protocol-mandated enhanced nights (entry, mid-stay, exit) plus one unannounced adaptive night per 2-week block, maintaining predictable staffing and reducing false-alarm response costs by 35% while accepting 40–45% event-capture miss rate.

Deploy reactive PSG escalation within 30 minutes of technician confirmation of a probable parasomnia event (based on video, sound, accelerometer threshold crossing, and chin EMG), funding a fourth night technician and on-call PSG technician rotation to maximize authentic event documentation and enable real-time intervention studies.

Implement a probabilistic overnight PSG schedule where 2–3 random nights per participant (beyond the three scheduled enhanced nights) are pre-assigned as full PSG nights during recruitment and consent, balancing cost predictability with improved event-capture diversity without reactive technician strain.
levers.review_lever: Controls cost-predictability vs. event-detection comprehensiveness. Weakness: The options underspecify how to handle inter-rater disagreement on whether technician-flagged accelerometer or microphone signals actually represent parasomnia (versus normal nocturnal movement), potentially triggering wasteful PSG escalations or allowing misclassified events into the dataset.

levers.name: Matched Control Recruitment Burden and Baseline Cohort Depth
levers.consequences: Immediate: Enrolling a matched control group (normal sleepers undergoing identical residential monitoring) requires parallel recruitment channels, lengthens project timeline, and absorbs €250–350K in participant compensation and staff time. Systemic: A 1:1 matched control arm with 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants (50–70 controls) increases statistical power for sensor false-positive benchmarking and event-triage algorithm specificity by 60–75%, but extends overall enrollment timeline by 6–9 months and diverts technician annotation effort from parasomnia data; conversely, a smaller control cohort (n=15–20) reduces cost by 40% and completes faster but leaves event-triage tool specificity unvalidated against healthy nocturnal behavior. Strategic: Control recruitment depth directly affects whether Aim 3 deliverable (benchmarked semi-automated tools) achieves publication-grade methodological rigor for commercial licensing or remains a preliminary proof-of-concept, shaping post-project licensing revenue and continuation funding.
options: Recruit and house a full matched control group (n=50–70) with identical 2–8 week residential stays, two independent scorers reviewing all control-night events, and comparative sensor-error analysis, extending the project timeline by 8 months but producing a publication-ready false-positive benchmark dataset.

Establish a smaller healthy-sleeper control cohort (n=20–25) undergoing abbreviated 4–7 day stays focused on sensor baseline calibration and artifact characterization rather than full-stay longitudinal follow-up, reducing cost by €180K and timeline impact to 3 months while accepting incomplete specificity validation for the event-triage tool.

Deploy a synthetic control strategy using historical polysomnography data from the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic (deidentified normal sleepers from routine clinical studies, n=30–40 nights) as a cost-free but potentially dated baseline for sensor false-positive rates, eliminating recruitment burden but introducing unmeasured sensor-drift and technical-protocol differences that could bias triage-tool benchmarking.
levers.review_lever: Controls methodological rigor vs. cost and timeline feasibility. Weakness: The options fail to account for seasonal variation in sleep architecture (winter vs. summer recruitment cohorts may show differing baseline arousal rates), which could render historical control data unreliable or require stratified matching by recruitment month.

levers.name: Night-Shift Technician Task Atomization and Annotation Workflow Automation
levers.consequences: Immediate: Deciding how much real-time vs. post-hoc annotation technicians perform during overnight shifts—and whether machine-assisted flagging (accelerometer thresholds, audio anomaly detection, automated video motion segmentation) pre-screens events—determines technician cognitive load and event-triage tool development pathway. Systemic: Requiring technicians to perform full AASM manual annotation during 8-hour night shifts (on top of safety monitoring and PSG setup) increases staffing fatigue, reduces per-technician productivity by ~20%, and requires 4 technicians instead of 3, adding €60K annually; conversely, deferring annotation to day-time scoring staff and using lightweight machine-flagging during night shifts reduces staffing to 3 technicians but requires hiring 2 additional full-time day-shift scorers and delays event-label availability for Aim 3 algorithm development by 3–4 weeks per cohort. Strategic: This tension between real-time human annotation (enabling rapid intervention and immediate feedback) versus deferred machine-assisted annotation (reducing operational cost but slowing data maturation) directly determines whether the facility can support concurrent Aim 2 longitudinal analysis and Aim 3 tool development, or must serialize them, affecting publication timeline and grant competitiveness.
options: Mandate that night technicians perform complete AASM-criteria manual annotation during live monitoring (using real-time video playback and EEG review), supplemented by a machine-flagging system (accelerometer z-score thresholds, audio spectral anomalies) as a secondary safety backup, accepting 4-person night staffing and higher fatigue risk but enabling immediate event verification and intervention capability.

Assign night technicians solely to safety monitoring, video-guided event detection, and lightweight preliminary event logging (time stamp, video clip, sensor channel), deferring all formal AASM annotation to day-shift trained scorers (2 FTE hires) who review de-identified clips within 24 hours, reducing night-shift cognitive burden by 60% and enabling dedicated 4-person day-shift annotation team for algorithm benchmarking.

Implement a hybrid model where night technicians perform real-time annotation only for escalated PSG nights (enhanced or flagged), while routine low-burden monitoring nights are queued for next-business-day annotation by day staff, reducing night technician overload by 40% while preserving real-time event-responsiveness on higher-acuity nights.
levers.review_lever: Controls human annotation fidelity vs. operational cost and staffing sustainability. Weakness: The options do not specify how discrepancies between real-time technician flagging and retrospective day-scorer consensus are arbitrated, potentially introducing systematic bias if night technicians over- or under-report events due to fatigue or attention lapses.

levers.name: RBD Secondary Protocol Clinical Depth and Neuro-Diagnostic Rigor Scope
levers.consequences: Immediate: The plan allocates a 'separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening' for REM sleep behavior disorder (a distinct parasomnia with higher neurodegenerative risk), requiring additional neurologist consultation, polysomnographic expertise, and ethics review. Systemic: A comprehensive RBD sub-protocol including baseline MRI, CSF biomarkers (alpha-synuclein, tau), and neuropsychology screening adds €350–500K to budget and extends ethics approval by 4–6 months, but enables Parkinson's disease risk stratification and produces high-impact co-publications in movement-disorder journals (estimated 40–60% citation premium); a minimalist RBD approach (clinical interview, standard PSG, no imaging) costs €80K and launches faster but yields only descriptive phenotyping without neuropathologic mechanistic insight. Strategic: RBD scientific depth directly affects competitive positioning for follow-on NIH/DFG grants (deep biomarker work attracts neurodegenerative-disease funding) versus staying narrowly focused on NREM parasomnia characterization (published faster but lower strategic impact), forcing a choice between early publications and sustained funding trajectory.
options: Conduct RBD protocol with comprehensive baseline neurologic assessment, brain MRI (3T, structural + diffusion-weighted imaging), lumbar puncture for CSF biomarkers, and neuropsychology battery, positioning the facility as a translational neurodegenerative-disease research hub with publications in high-impact movement-disorder journals.

Implement a focused RBD sub-protocol limited to clinical polysomnographic characterization (REM sleep without atonia quantification, seizure-like behavior documentation, motor event phasicity analysis) and neurologist interview, deferring biomarker and imaging work to post-hoc grant applications if early data warrants, minimizing upfront budget and complexity.

Defer RBD enrollment entirely to years 2–3 after NREM parasomnia operations and Aim 3 algorithm development are stabilized, allocating resources initially to NREM work only and adapting RBD scope based on clinician feedback and funding opportunities identified during pilot phase, reducing risk but sacrificing early competitive advantage in the neuroimmunology space.
levers.review_lever: Controls translational biomarker depth vs. budget conservatism and operational focus. Weakness: The options lack specification of whether RBD participants would remain in-facility for extended neuro-diagnostic procedures (CSF collection, MRI) or if those would be conducted off-site, affecting facility footprint planning and infection-control protocols.

levers.name: Publication-First vs. Data-Deposit-First Strategic Sequencing and IP Ownership Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: Deciding whether to publish Aims 1–2 findings first (methods, safety, longitudinal phenotyping) and deposit data second, or release de-identified physiological datasets immediately to a public repository (e.g., OpenNeuro, SLEEPDATA) while peer-review is pending, affects competitive advantage and scientific leadership narrative. Systemic: Publication-first sequencing (6–12 month embargo before data release) allows the core team to extract maximum discovery value and secure follow-on grants, but delays community access by 12–18 months and risks independent groups replicating work; data-first release (concurrent with pre-print or on acceptance) accelerates community benchmarking of Aim 3 tools by 6–9 months and attracts collaborative validation partners, but surrenders analytical priority and risks competitors improving the semi-automated tools faster than the team can publish. Strategic: This lever determines team reputation in the open-science community, eligibility for FAIR-data funding bonuses (DFG values open data), and the strength of follow-on grant applications—early data sharing signals methodological confidence but reduces publication novelty; delayed release signals competitive caution and may trigger funder criticism.
options: Publish all Aims 1–2–3 primary analyses and tool-development papers (anticipated 4–5 manuscripts) before depositing de-identified physiological and event-label datasets to OpenNeuro, maintaining a 12–18 month analytical exclusivity window and ensuring first-mover scientific narrative.

Deposit de-identified physiological data (excluding video) to OpenNeuro concurrently with first major Aim 1–2 methods paper submission (month 18–20), enabling parallel community validation of sensor performance and event-triage benchmarking while the team continues algorithm refinement and longer-term follow-up analysis.

Commit contractually to time-locked, pre-publication data release (month 24) via a dedicated institutional repository with data-use agreements mandating collaborative acknowledgment but not authorship, positioning the facility as a community data resource while preserving one-year post-hoc analytical advantage for the core team and licensing-related discoveries.
levers.review_lever: Controls competitive analytical advantage vs. open-science reputation and FAIR-data compliance. Weakness: The options do not address how early data release would affect licensing opportunities with pharmaceutical partners or whether restricted commercial-use licensing agreements are compatible with open-data mandates from DFG or journal policies.

levers.name: Facility Expansion Contingency and the 8-Bed-to-12-Bed Scaling Economics Trigger
levers.consequences: Immediate: The plan defers expansion from 8 to 12 active suites pending successful pilot outcomes; choosing when to pull the expansion trigger (at month 12 on soft metrics versus month 18 on hard event-capture thresholds versus never) determines lease renegotiation timing, renovations cost (€150–200K), and recruitment timeline acceleration. Systemic: Early expansion (month 12, based on enrollment velocity and participant satisfaction data) adds €180K operational cost annually (3 additional technician shifts, utilities, scoring staff) but could accelerate total cohort completion from 36 to 28 months, advancing publication timeline by 8 months and increasing competitive positioning; delayed or conditional expansion (month 18+, gated on ≥40 captured events meeting quality criteria) preserves €150K contingency reserve but risks losing recruited participants to waitlist frustration and reduces statistical power for Aim 2 sub-group analysis (RBD vs. sleepwalking vs. confusional arousals). Strategic: The expansion decision is a hidden proxy for risk tolerance—early expansion signals confidence in operational model and justifies continued funding, but late or no expansion signals caution and may undermine grant-renewal momentum, affecting whether DFG views this as a stable research infrastructure or a provisional pilot.
options: Commit to 12-bed expansion at month 12 if pilot cohort demonstrates ≥85% safety incident reporting compliance, ≥75% event-capture yield, and ≥4 independent scorer agreement on ≥70% of flagged events, investing €200K upfront and hiring two additional technicians to complete enrollment by month 32.

Implement a staged 8→10→12 expansion (adding 2 suites at month 12 and 2 additional at month 18) contingent on rolling success metrics (enrollment rate >2 participants/month, median event capture >3 events per participant by month 9), distributing cost and staffing impact across 18 months while maintaining flexibility to halt at 10-bed capacity if metrics deteriorate.

Maintain the 8-bed operational footprint for the full 36-month project, scaling productivity through technician efficiency gains and faster annotation turnaround rather than facility expansion, avoiding €200K capital cost and holding staffing stable, accepting a final cohort size of 40–50 (versus 50–70) and reduced sub-group statistical power for Aim 2.
levers.review_lever: Controls growth ambition vs. operational-risk management and budget certainty. Weakness: The options do not specify how expansion would affect residential property lease negotiations or whether the landlord has contractual flexibility to accommodate 12 suites if the property layout (plumbing, electrical capacity, fire egress) proves inadequate after pilot renovation, creating a hidden constraint on scaling.

responses.summary: The seven levers are well-balanced across facility design (ambiance vs. safety), data acquisition (tiered escalation PSG), team/workflow (technician annotation), secondary science (RBD depth), publication strategy (data sequencing), and growth (expansion trigger), each forcing explicit trade-offs between authenticity/depth and cost/speed. However, one critical dimension is missing: the governance and approval pathway for real-time technician safety decisions during high-risk events (e.g., participant wandering toward a window, signs of seizure-like activity). Add a new lever: 'Technician Event-Response Authority and Clinical Decision Escalation Protocol' with options spanning (1) empowering night technicians to intervene and de-escalate autonomously with post-hoc PI review, (2) requiring real-time telephone consultation with on-call physician for events exceeding predefined risk severity thresholds, and (3) deploying a part-time clinical psychologist or physician overnight on-site for months 1–6 pilot phase, then reverting to on-call model. This lever reveals the tension between operational speed (autonomous technician response) and liability/clinical oversight (physician decision authority), directly affecting insurance premiums, consent forms, and recruitment messaging.

levers.name: Domestic Ambiance vs. Clinical Safety Architecture
levers.consequences: Immediate: The facility adopts minimalist safety modifications (padded edges, restricted windows, door alarms) integrated into residential finishes rather than institutional-grade cladding, preserving a private-home aesthetic that encourages spontaneous parasomnia occurrence. Systemic: Reduced environmental suppression of behavior is predicted to increase captured-event yield by 15–25% versus overtly clinical settings, but creates staffing complexity—technicians must interpret safety risks in real time without institutional safeguards, raising incident reporting and liability documentation by an estimated 20–30% workload. Strategic: This trade-off determines whether the unit generates ecologically valid phenotypic data or becomes a sterile monitoring box that fails to capture naturalistic episodes, directly impacting Aim 2 success and publication competitiveness.
options: Pursue full residential ambiance with discrete safety integration, accepting higher real-time risk assessment burden on night technicians and requiring enhanced incident-response protocols and insurance provisions.

Adopt a hybrid aesthetic using clinical-grade safety elements (grab bars, commercial-grade padding) in bedrooms and monitored areas while maintaining residential finishes in common spaces, balancing naturalism with staff liability reduction.

Implement maximum clinical safety architecture with transparent acrylic barriers, institutional flooring, and visible monitoring infrastructure, accepting 15–20% reduction in spontaneous episode capture as the cost of unambiguous safety compliance.
levers.review: Controls ecological validity vs. institutional safety burden. Weakness: The options underspecify the actual occupant feedback loop—no mechanism is defined for participants to signal that an environment feels 'too clinical' or vice versa, so the chosen design may still suppress behavior despite intent.

levers.name: Event-Capture Yield and Stay Duration Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Participants begin with a 2–8 week baseline stay; those with zero captured events after the initial period are offered up to 2 extended weeks (capped at 10 weeks total), creating flexibility for sparse-episode participants but extending per-participant costs by an estimated €3,200–6,400 per extension week (staff, meals, facility overhead). Systemic: Allowing selective extension is projected to shift the unproductive-admission ceiling from the planned 20% to approximately 10–12%, capturing an additional 5–8 low-yield participants per cohort and generating 30–50 extra adjudicated events annually, but increases average stay length from 5 weeks to 6.5 weeks, reducing throughput from 30–35 to 24–28 annual admissions under fixed staffing. Strategic: This lever controls the Aim 2 event sample size and phenotypic diversity—higher yield at lower throughput produces richer longitudinal characterization but risks missing seasonal or trigger-based variation captured only through rapid participant rotation.
options: Enforce a fixed 2–4 week stay for all participants regardless of event capture, maximizing annual throughput (35–40 admissions) and reducing facility costs by €40K–60K annually, but accepting 25–30% unproductive admissions and weaker Aim 2 phenotypes.

Apply selective extension (current plan): offer 2 additional weeks to zero-event participants after the initial period, balancing yield improvement against throughput loss and requiring mid-stay re-consent and protocol amendment capacity.

Implement a tiered stay protocol where participants with ≥2 self-reported episodes per month begin with 1–3 weeks, those with 1 episode monthly start at 3 weeks, and those with <1 monthly begin at 4 weeks, targeting event capture predictively rather than reactively and reducing extensions by ~40% while maintaining higher yield.
levers.review: Controls statistical power (sample size and events per person) vs. annual throughput and budget efficiency. Weakness: The tiered entry strategy assumes reliable self-reported episode frequency at baseline, yet parasomnia recall is notoriously poor; misclassification at enrollment could waste stays on participants unlikely to produce events.

levers.name: Night-Shift Staffing and Event-Response Coverage
levers.consequences: Immediate: The current plan deploys 3 full-time technicians rotating to cover 8 active suites (2.67 suites per technician during overnight hours), allowing 1 technician to attend to a flagged event while 2 remain available for monitoring and annotation, with a 30% surge buffer built in. Systemic: This ratio is reported to generate an estimated incident-response time of 2–3 minutes and false-alarm review burden of 8–12 hours per technician per week; if suite utilization reaches 10–12 beds, the ratio degrades to 3.3–4 suites per technician, increasing response time to 4–6 minutes and false-alarm review to 15–20 hours weekly, which approaches technician burnout thresholds and correlates with annotation error rates rising 8–12%. Strategic: Staffing scalability directly constrains facility expansion—the decision to expand to 12 suites by year two or remain at 8 units hinge on whether technician workload remains sustainable, affecting year-three revenue, Aim 2 event capture, and the unit's viability as a standalone operation.
options: Maintain 3-technician staffing and cap the facility at 8 active suites permanently, ensuring sustainable incident response and annotation quality but limiting annual admissions to 24–28 participants and raising per-participant operational cost by 18–25% versus scaled operations.

Recruit a fourth technician by month 12 and expand to 10–11 active suites in year two, maintaining a 2.5–2.75 suite-per-technician ratio, adding approximately €80K in annual salary and benefits but enabling 35–40 annual admissions and better cost-per-event recovery.

Implement a hybrid model combining full-time technicians (3 FTE) with cross-trained clinical psychology interns or postdoctoral researchers for supplemental evening coverage and annotation review, reducing per-unit technician cost by 20–30% but introducing variability in event-response expertise and annotation consistency.
levers.review: Controls staffing scalability and operational sustainability vs. per-participant cost and event-capture yield. Weakness: The options do not address technician retention and burnout risk under high false-alarm loads—no mention of administrative support, annotation automation, or mental health resources for night-shift workers.

levers.name: Sensor Tiering and Data-Collection Burden
levers.consequences: Immediate: The plan employs a three-tier model—nightly low-burden monitoring (dry EEG headband, contact-free mattress, infrared camera, ambient sensors) plus scheduled enhanced nights (full PSG on nights 1, mid-stay, and final) plus escalation nights (full PSG after technician-flagged events). Systemic: This approach is projected to yield a physiological dataset with 95%+ temporal coverage (continuous movement, environmental data) paired with clinical-grade EEG on approximately 15–20% of recording hours (enhanced plus escalation nights), reducing per-participant PSG cost by 40–50% (estimated €600–800 per participant) versus nightly full PSG, yet generates a heterogeneous annotation burden—technicians must score low-resolution events on some nights and high-resolution events on others, introducing inter-night scoring variability of approximately 8–15%. Strategic: This tiering controls both the event-triage algorithm's training dataset composition and the Aim 2 phenotypic characterization depth; too much low-resolution data weakens the algorithm's sensitivity, while too much PSG exhausts budget and overstaffs the scoring pipeline.
options: Adopt nightly full PSG across all participants for maximum physiological resolution and algorithmic training power, enabling the most robust Aim 3 event-triage tool but consuming 60–70% of the 3-year equipment and staff budget on data acquisition, severely constraining Aim 2 sample size to 20–25 total participants.

Implement the tiered model as planned (low-burden nightly plus scheduled and escalation enhancements), accepting data heterogeneity and inter-night annotation variability while optimizing budget and sample size, requiring explicit sensitivity-specificity trade-offs in the Aim 3 algorithm.

Reverse the hierarchy: deploy nightly full PSG only for the first 2 weeks of each participant's stay, then transition to low-burden monitoring thereafter, capturing acute-entry effects and event clustering in the initial period with clinical-grade data while reducing costs in later weeks by 35–45%, but losing long-term within-person physiological stability information.
levers.review: Controls physiological resolution and algorithm training quality vs. per-participant cost and sample-size feasibility. Weakness: The options assume the low-burden sensors (dry-electrode EEG, contact-free mattress) will generate sufficient signal quality for preliminary event detection, but this is unvalidated in a 2–8 week continuous-monitoring context; sensor drift or participant adaptation could degrade low-burden data quality by month 3 of operation.

levers.name: Participant Recruitment Filtering and Pre-Admission Validation
levers.consequences: Immediate: The protocol mandates a structured pre-admission pathway including specialist clinical interview, collateral history, prior/screening PSG, and exclusion-checklist adjudication by the PI, which reduces enrollment speed but gates inappropriate admissions (active substance use, untreated OSA, epilepsy mimics). Systemic: Strict pre-admission screening is predicted to reject 20–30% of self-referred candidates, extending recruitment time by 4–8 weeks per target cohort and lowering conversion from initial inquiry to signed consent by approximately 15–20% percentage points, but reducing in-facility safety incidents and false-parasomnia diagnoses by an estimated 25–35%, thereby improving Aim 2 data quality and publication credibility. Strategic: This lever controls both the recruitment timeline and the statistical homogeneity of the parasomnia phenotypes; loose screening accelerates enrollment but conflates distinct sleep disorders (e.g., confusional arousals misdiagnosed as sleepwalking), while tight screening delays the study but yields cleaner Aim 2 longitudinal characterization.
options: Adopt a simplified phone-screening model followed by in-person PSG only, reducing pre-admission time to 2–3 weeks and improving recruitment flow by 25%, but accepting 20–25% enrollment of non-parasomnia sleep disorders or medication-induced confusional states that later require protocol amendment or exclusion.

Maintain the current structured pre-admission pathway with PI adjudication, requiring 6–8 weeks from inquiry to enrollment, with dual external review for borderline cases to improve diagnostic confidence, slowing recruitment by 30–40% but achieving high diagnostic specificity and minimal in-facility protocol violations.

Implement a tiered recruitment model: fast-track clear-cut sleepwalking cases through abbreviated screening (3 weeks), while reserving full pre-admission workup for ambiguous cases (sleep terrors without clear motor components, rare phenotypes), maintaining 6–month recruitment target through case-mix stratification.
levers.review: Controls diagnostic specificity and in-facility safety vs. recruitment velocity and enrollment rate. Weakness: The options overlook the reliance on collateral history from bed partners or household members, which may be unavailable for single-occupancy participants or those unwilling to disclose sleep behaviors; this could introduce silent misclassification of non-parasomnia nocturnal behaviors.

levers.name: Data Governance and Public Repository Participation
levers.consequences: Immediate: The protocol limits public data sharing to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements (DVAs), while bedroom video is retained privately due to severe privacy constraints and not deposited in public repositories. Systemic: This approach preserves participant privacy and reduces legal/regulatory overhead (estimated €40K–60K in additional GDPR and ethics review work if video were shared), but restricts academic impact and algorithmic transparency—external researchers cannot fully replicate event-triage scoring decisions without access to the video ground truth, potentially limiting downstream algorithm adoption and citation impact by an estimated 30–40% compared to fully open datasets. Strategic: This lever controls the replicability, transparency, and long-term influence of the Aim 3 event-triage tool; restrictive data governance protects participants but may prevent the algorithm from becoming a community resource or clinical standard.
options: Pursue full public data deposition including pixel-level de-identified or pixelated video (e.g., silhouettes only, geometric masks) alongside EEG and sensor streams, requiring enhanced consent processes, video anonymization validation, and 12–18 month ethics and legal review, but enabling full methodological transparency and maximum academic impact for the Aim 3 algorithm.

Maintain the current restricted-sharing model with DVA-protected physiological data only, preserving privacy with minimal overhead and allowing the algorithm to be shared under controlled licensing (e.g., academic research licenses, institutional collaborations), accepting reduced transparency and a narrower secondary-research footprint.

Adopt a hybrid time-locked release strategy: keep video private indefinitely, but deposit EEG, sensor, and manually adjudicated event-label data in a public repository after a 2-year embargo, allowing early users to develop and benchmark algorithms against the sensor and manual-score data, then release additional cohorts' data progressively, balancing privacy and impact.
levers.review: Controls participant privacy protection vs. algorithmic transparency and community adoption. Weakness: The options do not specify how the de-identification validation of video (if released) will be audited for re-identification risk or how participants' rights to view or revoke their own data will be implemented under GDPR.

levers.name: Event-Triage Algorithm Endpoint and Clinical Utility
levers.consequences: Immediate: The protocol specifies that the Aim 3 event-triage tool will rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising independent diagnosis. Systemic: This conservative framing protects against overstatement of algorithmic capability (typical failure mode of early sleep informatics), but limits the tool's immediate clinical utility—the algorithm becomes a technician's 'filter' (e.g., 'flag top 5% of movement events for EEG-based scoring review') rather than a standalone diagnostic aid, reducing adoption friction in the facility but also reducing its commercial attractiveness or clinical translation likelihood by an estimated 40–50%, and capping publications in high-impact venues to methods-validation journals rather than clinical-application journals. Strategic: This lever determines whether the Aim 3 deliverable becomes a published methodological contribution or a stepping stone toward a phase-two intervention or diagnostic-validation study; the choice shapes the entire grant-funding narrative for year four.
options: Position the algorithm strictly as a review-prioritization tool, publishing Aim 3 results in sleep medicine methods journals with sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, explicitly disclaiming independent diagnostic capability and targeting adoption by research technicians within the facility and cognate research units.

Develop the algorithm with a dual-pathway design: a low-threshold 'event-candidate filter' for reducing technician annotation burden (published in methods venues) plus a higher-confidence subset of predictions paired with confidence intervals, proposing this higher-confidence pathway for future validation studies as a potential clinical-decision-support input (setting up phase-two funding).

Train the algorithm to estimate event-type probabilities (e.g., 'sleepwalking vs. confusional arousal vs. sleep terror' posterior distributions) and benchmark these probabilities against multimodal expert consensus (video + EEG + clinical history), framing Aim 3 as a phenotypic-stratification tool rather than a binary event detector, enabling preliminary claims about parasomnia subtyping that could attract pharmaceutical collaborators interested in drug-specific phenotype enrichment.
levers.review: Controls algorithmic scope and clinical-translation risk vs. publication impact and pharma partnership attraction. Weakness: The options assume the dual human scorers will achieve sufficient inter-rater agreement (typically ICC >0.75) to serve as a ground-truth reference; if scoring agreement is poor (ICC 0.5–0.65), the algorithm's benchmark becomes contaminated and its clinical utility claim becomes self-referential.

levers.name: Pre-Admission Clinical Rigor Versus Recruitment Velocity
levers.consequences: Immediate: Stricter pre-admission screening (e.g., requiring two independent sleep clinic assessments plus actigraphy documentation) eliminates lower-confidence cases but lengthens recruitment cycles by an estimated 3–4 weeks per cohort. Systemic: Tighter inclusion reduces zero-event admissions by ~8–12 percentage points (from 20% to 8–10%), improving cost-per-adjudicated-event by approximately 18–22%, but extends year-one enrollment from 20 to 14–16 participants. Strategic: Higher diagnostic confidence at admission strengthens publication claims and reduces protocol deviations, but delays Aim 2 sample accumulation and risks missing the 50-participant target by month 24.
options: Conduct single in-person clinical assessment at University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic with structured questionnaire, prior polysomnography review, and bed-partner interview, accepting 18–22% unproductive admissions as baseline.

Require two independent specialist evaluations (sleep medicine and neurology) plus minimum 2 weeks of actigraphy baseline showing consistent parasomnia patterns, reducing unproductive admissions to 8–10% but delaying recruitment by 4–6 weeks.

Deploy a tiered rapid-access pathway: first-line telephonic screening by the study coordinator using standardized ICSD-3 symptom algorithm, conditional in-person assessment only for borderline cases, balancing speed with modest quality trade-off.
levers.review: Controls recruitment speed vs. diagnostic confidence. Weakness: The options do not address how pre-admission delays interact with participant availability windows and seasonal variation in sleep disorder clinic referral patterns.

levers.name: Longitudinal Retention Engagement Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: Participants admitted for 2–8 week stays face monotony, separation anxiety, and motivation drift after week 3, risking early protocol withdrawal. Systemic: A structured engagement protocol (mid-stay psychologist check-ins, goal-setting interviews, simplified daily feedback on event detection) could reduce dropout by 5–8 percentage points (~3–5 early withdrawals prevented per cohort of 30), recovering approximately €12K–18K in participant compensation savings. Strategic: Lower attrition ensures minimum stay durations are reached, enabling longitudinal clustering analysis and reducing sample-size inflation needed to compensate for incomplete trajectories.
options: Deliver a single orientation session at admission and weekly optional group dinners with study staff, minimizing intervention burden but accepting baseline 12–15% withdrawal rates.

Implement twice-weekly structured psychologist check-ins covering sleep anxiety, home separation coping, and real-time event feedback, with a personalized weekly progress email from the PI summarizing detected episodes and phenotypic insights.

Introduce a participant research dashboard accessible via secure portal showing real-time event counts, comparative phenotyping metrics against their own baseline, and milestone celebrations (e.g., 'Event #5 captured'), gamifying engagement without introducing external reward systems.
levers.review: Controls engagement intensity vs. technician and psychologist time allocation. Weakness: The options fail to distinguish between retention in early weeks (amenable to psychologist contact) versus late-stay engagement (where fatigue and diminishing-novelty factors may dominate irreversibly).

levers.name: Event-Triage Algorithm Automation Scope and Validation Pathway
levers.consequences: Immediate: Automating event-flagging (e.g., kinematic thresholds from mattress sensors + microphone vocalizations) reduces technician real-time annotation workload by 40–50% per night. Systemic: A semi-automated algorithm ranked at 85–90% sensitivity but 60–70% specificity (relative to dual human scorer consensus) generates candidate-event lists requiring technician review, yielding a net 25–30% reduction in total manual review hours versus purely manual screening, but introduces false-positive review overhead. Strategic: Moderate automation achieves Aim 3 deliverables (benchmarked triage tools) within computational scope, yet aggressive automation (>90% specificity) risks missing rare morphologies and delays algorithm development into year three.
options: Restrict automation to asynchronous overnight flagging: mattress + audio sensors trigger automated alerts, technicians confirm during real-time monitoring, two independent raters score only high-confidence flagged intervals, preserving human oversight but accepting lower flag specificity.

Deploy a semi-supervised ensemble (decision-tree classifier on kinematic + audio features trained on 10–15 manually labeled participant-weeks) ranked by confidence score, with technicians reviewing only top-ranked candidates during night monitoring and retrospective scoring on all flagged segments.

Develop a Bayesian hierarchical model incorporating participant-specific baseline motion patterns, circadian timing of parasomnia onset, and multi-modal sensor fusion, aiming for 88–92% sensitivity at 75–80% specificity and positioning the tool as a prospective clinical decision-aid in phase-two trials.
levers.review: Controls computational ambition vs. manual-review sustainability. Weakness: The options underspecify how false positives (e.g., restless sleep, violent dream content) are distinguished from true parasomnia morphologies without subjective technician judgment, and do not address annotation drift when dual raters work across 18-month timescales.

levers.name: Night-Shift Staffing Elasticity and Event-Response Readiness
levers.consequences: Immediate: Fixed 3-technician rotation covering 8 suites (2.67 suites per technician per night) leaves minimal bandwidth for real-time event response, extended annotation, or sick-leave coverage. Systemic: Expanding to 4 technicians improves suite-to-staff ratio to 2.0:1, reducing response time by ~3–5 minutes per flagged event and enabling concurrent annotation of multiple events; estimated cost increase is €45K–55K per year (salary + benefits), raising personnel budgets by ~6–7%. Strategic: Better staffing elasticity improves event-capture quality and safety margins, but accelerates year-two budget commitment and constrains funding flexibility for computational infrastructure or algorithm development.
