options: Maintain 3-technician roster with cross-training on event recognition and data-pipeline troubleshooting; deploy per-diem casual staff (registered polysomnographers) on call for event surges, accepting 8–15 minute event-response latency and bottleneck risk when multiple simultaneous flags occur.

Hire 4 full-time technicians structured as 2-on/2-off rotation, guaranteeing two independent observers per night shift and enabling real-time event documentation with <3 minute response latency; lock €50K incremental budget in year two.

Adopt a flexible model with 2 permanent full-time technicians (core coverage) plus 1–2 shift-variable technicians (10–20 hours/week) scheduled around predicted high-event-yield periods (e.g., participant weeks 2–4 post-adaptation), optimizing cost-per-event without sacrificing response speed on average.
levers.review: Controls safety margins vs. operational expense. Weakness: The options do not model how event-response latency (minutes 0–15) affects physiological signal quality for retrospective scoring and whether faster response genuinely improves Aim 3 algorithm training or merely yields redundant video documentation.

levers.name: Facility Footprint Scaling and Lease Economics
levers.consequences: Immediate: The current lease secures 8 active suites with capacity for 4 additional suites (total 12) in the same property; expansion to 12 suites requires renovation investment (~€180K–220K) and increased utility costs (~€8K–12K/year). Systemic: Expanding to 12 suites by month 18 increases annual participant throughput capacity from ~35 to ~50 participants/year, reducing cost-per-participant-admitted by ~15–18% (amortized across fixed overhead), but only if recruitment channels sustain 40–50 admissions/year rather than the baseline 30–35. Strategic: Early expansion locks in lease-term favorable terms and demonstrates progress to funding boards, but risks stranded capacity if enrollment plateaus; deferral preserves optionality and conserves year-two capital but may require a second lease negotiation at higher market rates if expansion becomes urgent by month 24.
options: Renovate 4 additional suites immediately upon year-one participant stabilization (month 6–8), deploy to production by month 12, projecting 12-suite operations by mid-year-two and enabling 50+ annual admissions.

Defer physical expansion beyond the 8-suite baseline through year two; conduct a month-18 enrollment-rate review and trigger 4-suite renovation only if 25+ participants have been enrolled with consistent inter-admission intervals <2 weeks.

Negotiate a separate lease for a second smaller property (4 suites) in an adjacent Bonn neighborhood, distributing geographic footprint and reducing single-site operational risk, but incurring dual-site logistics and requiring two separate night-shift teams (~€35K additional annual cost).
levers.review: Controls capital efficiency vs. operational flexibility. Weakness: The options ignore how dual-site operations (if chosen) affect inter-rater reliability training, centralized data-pipeline management, and the ability of a single PI to maintain oversight across dispersed facilities.

levers.name: Inter-Rater Reliability Governance and Annotation Protocol Consistency
levers.consequences: Immediate: Two independent raters score all flagged events using AASM criteria with discordance logged; unresolved disagreements (kappa <0.75 on subscale) trigger a third expert referee or protocol clarification. Systemic: Implementing quarterly calibration sessions (both raters score the same 5–10 reference events and discuss discordance patterns) maintains inter-rater reliability above κ=0.80 across the 18-month scoring window, estimated to cost €8K/year in senior sleep-medicine physician time. Strategic: Rigorous inter-rater governance strengthens publication credibility and Aim 3 algorithm benchmarking, but adds administrative overhead and risks publication delays if referee discordance is frequent enough to warrant protocol amendments.
options: Two independent raters score all events with discordance recorded; unresolved disagreements are marked as inconclusive, excluded from Aim 2 analysis, and reported as a sensitivity estimate in methods.

Conduct quarterly joint-scoring calibration sessions with 10 reference events per session; track kappa by rater-pair and event morphology; introduce a standing third-rater protocol if rolling 30-event kappa drops below 0.78, with consensus scoring and root-cause analysis (fatigue, protocol drift, ambiguous morphology).

Employ a single expert (senior sleep neurophysiologist at University Hospital Bonn, blinded to participant identity) as primary scorer, with secondary verification by the postdoc on 20% random sample; discordance triggers discussion and protocol refinement, reducing overhead but concentrating decision authority.
levers.review: Controls annotation consistency vs. resource expenditure. Weakness: The options do not specify how seasonal changes in rater availability (e.g., clinical leave, grant deadlines) or the accumulation of borderline cases late in the project cycle affect scoring backlog and publication timelines.

levers.name: External Scientific Visibility and Publication Sequencing Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Delaying major publications until month 24 (after Aim 2 and Aim 3 completion) maximizes comprehensive findings but risks competitor pre-emption and funder perception of slow progress. Systemic: A staged publication model (methods validation at month 12, preliminary phenotyping at month 18, algorithm benchmarking at month 24) requires 3–4 concurrent manuscripts in preparation, increasing PI and postdoc writing burden by ~15–20% annually but distributing evidence-of-progress across two funding-report cycles. Strategic: Early partial publications accelerate scientific impact and bolster year-two funding renewals, but may commit preliminary findings to print before full dataset maturation, risking critiques of incomplete sample size or missed confounders.
options: Reserve all publications for the 24–36 month window; submit a single comprehensive methods + phenotyping + algorithm paper at month 28, prioritizing depth over visibility and avoiding preliminary-findings criticism.

Publish a methods-validation manuscript at month 12 (residential capture safety, data quality, inter-rater reliability), a longitudinal-characterization paper at month 20 (first 40–50 participants, within-subject clustering, phenotypic subgroups), and an algorithm-benchmarking paper at month 30.

Deploy rapid-turnaround preprints beginning at month 9 (preliminary safety data), followed by peer-reviewed methods paper at month 14 and a living-document preprint series tracking algorithm development in parallel with manuscript preparation, maximizing early-stage visibility and inviting community feedback.
levers.review: Controls publication urgency vs. scientific maturity. Weakness: The options do not account for journal review timelines (3–6 months peer review + revision), which may decouple planned submission dates from actual publication dates and complicate funder-narrative alignment across years two and three.

levers.name: Domestic Materiality Calibration and Behavioral Naturalism Risk
levers.consequences: Immediate: Choosing heavy residential décor and sound dampening versus minimal clinical fixtures directly alters how participants move, sleep position, and arouse during night monitoring. Systemic: Overly domestic settings reduce technician vigilance and event-detection latency by an estimated 15–25%, increasing missed-event risk and delaying clinical response, while overly clinical décor suppresses parasomnia expression by 20–35% based on environment-behavior literature in sleep disorders. Strategic: The tension between ecological validity and safety assurance determines whether the facility captures authentic parasomnia phenotypes or merely documents artifact-laden behavior in an unfamiliar setting, directly affecting Aim 2 longitudinal characterization and publication credibility.
options: Design the residential core (bedrooms, hallways, living areas) with full domestic furnishings, warm lighting, personal artwork, and family-grade soft goods, accepting that technicians must rotate through a formal safety checklist every 2 hours to maintain event-response readiness.

Implement a hybrid model with domestic sleeping quarters but a dedicated clinical observation and intervention zone (adjacent safety room with monitors, crash cart, panic buttons) where technicians remain stationed during high-risk hours (22:00–06:00), preserving bedroom authenticity while centralizing safety control.

Adopt a minimalist, safety-optimized interior with permanent clinical-grade fixtures (washable surfaces, fixed furniture, medical-standard alarm systems), relying on subtle acoustic and lighting design to approximate residential comfort while accepting a 15–20% reduction in event yield as the trade-off for maximum technician efficiency and incident prevention.
levers.review: Controls environment authenticity vs. clinical safety optimization. Weakness: The options do not address how facility ambiance affects long-term participant retention or the cost of retrofitting if the pilot cohort reports psychological dissatisfaction with their assigned setting.

levers.name: Escalation Polysomnography Trigger Design and Event-Capture Sequencing
levers.consequences: Immediate: Defining when to deploy full clinical-grade PSG (scheduled enhanced nights on days 1, mid-stay, final day versus technician-flagged reactive escalation versus random adaptive scheduling) determines staffing burden and equipment utilization. Systemic: Scheduled-only PSG reduces equipment cost by ~€120K over 3 years and technician overtime by 30% but misses 40–50% of infrequent parasomnia episodes; reactive escalation increases capture sensitivity to 75–85% but requires 2 additional night technicians (€180K salary overhead) and unpredictable after-hours PSG setup costs of €60–90K annually. Strategic: This lever determines whether the project can fund Aim 3 (semi-automated event-triage benchmarking) with sufficient event-label density, or whether Aim 2 will be underpowered for temporal clustering analysis, directly affecting publication scope and grant renewal viability.
options: Reserve full PSG to protocol-mandated enhanced nights (entry, mid-stay, exit) plus one unannounced adaptive night per 2-week block, maintaining predictable staffing and reducing false-alarm response costs by 35% while accepting 40–45% event-capture miss rate.

Deploy reactive PSG escalation within 30 minutes of technician confirmation of a probable parasomnia event (based on video, sound, accelerometer threshold crossing, and chin EMG), funding a fourth night technician and on-call PSG technician rotation to maximize authentic event documentation and enable real-time intervention studies.

Implement a probabilistic overnight PSG schedule where 2–3 random nights per participant (beyond the three scheduled enhanced nights) are pre-assigned as full PSG nights during recruitment and consent, balancing cost predictability with improved event-capture diversity without reactive technician strain.
levers.review: Controls cost-predictability vs. event-detection comprehensiveness. Weakness: The options underspecify how to handle inter-rater disagreement on whether technician-flagged accelerometer or microphone signals actually represent parasomnia (versus normal nocturnal movement), potentially triggering wasteful PSG escalations or allowing misclassified events into the dataset.

levers.name: Matched Control Recruitment Burden and Baseline Cohort Depth
levers.consequences: Immediate: Enrolling a matched control group (normal sleepers undergoing identical residential monitoring) requires parallel recruitment channels, lengthens project timeline, and absorbs €250–350K in participant compensation and staff time. Systemic: A 1:1 matched control arm with 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants (50–70 controls) increases statistical power for sensor false-positive benchmarking and event-triage algorithm specificity by 60–75%, but extends overall enrollment timeline by 6–9 months and diverts technician annotation effort from parasomnia data; conversely, a smaller control cohort (n=15–20) reduces cost by 40% and completes faster but leaves event-triage tool specificity unvalidated against healthy nocturnal behavior. Strategic: Control recruitment depth directly affects whether Aim 3 deliverable (benchmarked semi-automated tools) achieves publication-grade methodological rigor for commercial licensing or remains a preliminary proof-of-concept, shaping post-project licensing revenue and continuation funding.
options: Recruit and house a full matched control group (n=50–70) with identical 2–8 week residential stays, two independent scorers reviewing all control-night events, and comparative sensor-error analysis, extending the project timeline by 8 months but producing a publication-ready false-positive benchmark dataset.

Establish a smaller healthy-sleeper control cohort (n=20–25) undergoing abbreviated 4–7 day stays focused on sensor baseline calibration and artifact characterization rather than full-stay longitudinal follow-up, reducing cost by €180K and timeline impact to 3 months while accepting incomplete specificity validation for the event-triage tool.

Deploy a synthetic control strategy using historical polysomnography data from the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic (deidentified normal sleepers from routine clinical studies, n=30–40 nights) as a cost-free but potentially dated baseline for sensor false-positive rates, eliminating recruitment burden but introducing unmeasured sensor-drift and technical-protocol differences that could bias triage-tool benchmarking.
levers.review: Controls methodological rigor vs. cost and timeline feasibility. Weakness: The options fail to account for seasonal variation in sleep architecture (winter vs. summer recruitment cohorts may show differing baseline arousal rates), which could render historical control data unreliable or require stratified matching by recruitment month.

levers.name: Night-Shift Technician Task Atomization and Annotation Workflow Automation
levers.consequences: Immediate: Deciding how much real-time vs. post-hoc annotation technicians perform during overnight shifts—and whether machine-assisted flagging (accelerometer thresholds, audio anomaly detection, automated video motion segmentation) pre-screens events—determines technician cognitive load and event-triage tool development pathway. Systemic: Requiring technicians to perform full AASM manual annotation during 8-hour night shifts (on top of safety monitoring and PSG setup) increases staffing fatigue, reduces per-technician productivity by ~20%, and requires 4 technicians instead of 3, adding €60K annually; conversely, deferring annotation to day-time scoring staff and using lightweight machine-flagging during night shifts reduces staffing to 3 technicians but requires hiring 2 additional full-time day-shift scorers and delays event-label availability for Aim 3 algorithm development by 3–4 weeks per cohort. Strategic: This tension between real-time human annotation (enabling rapid intervention and immediate feedback) versus deferred machine-assisted annotation (reducing operational cost but slowing data maturation) directly determines whether the facility can support concurrent Aim 2 longitudinal analysis and Aim 3 tool development, or must serialize them, affecting publication timeline and grant competitiveness.
options: Mandate that night technicians perform complete AASM-criteria manual annotation during live monitoring (using real-time video playback and EEG review), supplemented by a machine-flagging system (accelerometer z-score thresholds, audio spectral anomalies) as a secondary safety backup, accepting 4-person night staffing and higher fatigue risk but enabling immediate event verification and intervention capability.

Assign night technicians solely to safety monitoring, video-guided event detection, and lightweight preliminary event logging (time stamp, video clip, sensor channel), deferring all formal AASM annotation to day-shift trained scorers (2 FTE hires) who review de-identified clips within 24 hours, reducing night-shift cognitive burden by 60% and enabling dedicated 4-person day-shift annotation team for algorithm benchmarking.

Implement a hybrid model where night technicians perform real-time annotation only for escalated PSG nights (enhanced or flagged), while routine low-burden monitoring nights are queued for next-business-day annotation by day staff, reducing night technician overload by 40% while preserving real-time event-responsiveness on higher-acuity nights.
levers.review: Controls human annotation fidelity vs. operational cost and staffing sustainability. Weakness: The options do not specify how discrepancies between real-time technician flagging and retrospective day-scorer consensus are arbitrated, potentially introducing systematic bias if night technicians over- or under-report events due to fatigue or attention lapses.

levers.name: RBD Secondary Protocol Clinical Depth and Neuro-Diagnostic Rigor Scope
levers.consequences: Immediate: The plan allocates a 'separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening' for REM sleep behavior disorder (a distinct parasomnia with higher neurodegenerative risk), requiring additional neurologist consultation, polysomnographic expertise, and ethics review. Systemic: A comprehensive RBD sub-protocol including baseline MRI, CSF biomarkers (alpha-synuclein, tau), and neuropsychology screening adds €350–500K to budget and extends ethics approval by 4–6 months, but enables Parkinson's disease risk stratification and produces high-impact co-publications in movement-disorder journals (estimated 40–60% citation premium); a minimalist RBD approach (clinical interview, standard PSG, no imaging) costs €80K and launches faster but yields only descriptive phenotyping without neuropathologic mechanistic insight. Strategic: RBD scientific depth directly affects competitive positioning for follow-on NIH/DFG grants (deep biomarker work attracts neurodegenerative-disease funding) versus staying narrowly focused on NREM parasomnia characterization (published faster but lower strategic impact), forcing a choice between early publications and sustained funding trajectory.
options: Conduct RBD protocol with comprehensive baseline neurologic assessment, brain MRI (3T, structural + diffusion-weighted imaging), lumbar puncture for CSF biomarkers, and neuropsychology battery, positioning the facility as a translational neurodegenerative-disease research hub with publications in high-impact movement-disorder journals.

Implement a focused RBD sub-protocol limited to clinical polysomnographic characterization (REM sleep without atonia quantification, seizure-like behavior documentation, motor event phasicity analysis) and neurologist interview, deferring biomarker and imaging work to post-hoc grant applications if early data warrants, minimizing upfront budget and complexity.

Defer RBD enrollment entirely to years 2–3 after NREM parasomnia operations and Aim 3 algorithm development are stabilized, allocating resources initially to NREM work only and adapting RBD scope based on clinician feedback and funding opportunities identified during pilot phase, reducing risk but sacrificing early competitive advantage in the neuroimmunology space.
levers.review: Controls translational biomarker depth vs. budget conservatism and operational focus. Weakness: The options lack specification of whether RBD participants would remain in-facility for extended neuro-diagnostic procedures (CSF collection, MRI) or if those would be conducted off-site, affecting facility footprint planning and infection-control protocols.

levers.name: Publication-First vs. Data-Deposit-First Strategic Sequencing and IP Ownership Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: Deciding whether to publish Aims 1–2 findings first (methods, safety, longitudinal phenotyping) and deposit data second, or release de-identified physiological datasets immediately to a public repository (e.g., OpenNeuro, SLEEPDATA) while peer-review is pending, affects competitive advantage and scientific leadership narrative. Systemic: Publication-first sequencing (6–12 month embargo before data release) allows the core team to extract maximum discovery value and secure follow-on grants, but delays community access by 12–18 months and risks independent groups replicating work; data-first release (concurrent with pre-print or on acceptance) accelerates community benchmarking of Aim 3 tools by 6–9 months and attracts collaborative validation partners, but surrenders analytical priority and risks competitors improving the semi-automated tools faster than the team can publish. Strategic: This lever determines team reputation in the open-science community, eligibility for FAIR-data funding bonuses (DFG values open data), and the strength of follow-on grant applications—early data sharing signals methodological confidence but reduces publication novelty; delayed release signals competitive caution and may trigger funder criticism.
options: Publish all Aims 1–2–3 primary analyses and tool-development papers (anticipated 4–5 manuscripts) before depositing de-identified physiological and event-label datasets to OpenNeuro, maintaining a 12–18 month analytical exclusivity window and ensuring first-mover scientific narrative.

Deposit de-identified physiological data (excluding video) to OpenNeuro concurrently with first major Aim 1–2 methods paper submission (month 18–20), enabling parallel community validation of sensor performance and event-triage benchmarking while the team continues algorithm refinement and longer-term follow-up analysis.

Commit contractually to time-locked, pre-publication data release (month 24) via a dedicated institutional repository with data-use agreements mandating collaborative acknowledgment but not authorship, positioning the facility as a community data resource while preserving one-year post-hoc analytical advantage for the core team and licensing-related discoveries.
levers.review: Controls competitive analytical advantage vs. open-science reputation and FAIR-data compliance. Weakness: The options do not address how early data release would affect licensing opportunities with pharmaceutical partners or whether restricted commercial-use licensing agreements are compatible with open-data mandates from DFG or journal policies.

levers.name: Facility Expansion Contingency and the 8-Bed-to-12-Bed Scaling Economics Trigger
levers.consequences: Immediate: The plan defers expansion from 8 to 12 active suites pending successful pilot outcomes; choosing when to pull the expansion trigger (at month 12 on soft metrics versus month 18 on hard event-capture thresholds versus never) determines lease renegotiation timing, renovations cost (€150–200K), and recruitment timeline acceleration. Systemic: Early expansion (month 12, based on enrollment velocity and participant satisfaction data) adds €180K operational cost annually (3 additional technician shifts, utilities, scoring staff) but could accelerate total cohort completion from 36 to 28 months, advancing publication timeline by 8 months and increasing competitive positioning; delayed or conditional expansion (month 18+, gated on ≥40 captured events meeting quality criteria) preserves €150K contingency reserve but risks losing recruited participants to waitlist frustration and reduces statistical power for Aim 2 sub-group analysis (RBD vs. sleepwalking vs. confusional arousals). Strategic: The expansion decision is a hidden proxy for risk tolerance—early expansion signals confidence in operational model and justifies continued funding, but late or no expansion signals caution and may undermine grant-renewal momentum, affecting whether DFG views this as a stable research infrastructure or a provisional pilot.
options: Commit to 12-bed expansion at month 12 if pilot cohort demonstrates ≥85% safety incident reporting compliance, ≥75% event-capture yield, and ≥4 independent scorer agreement on ≥70% of flagged events, investing €200K upfront and hiring two additional technicians to complete enrollment by month 32.

Implement a staged 8→10→12 expansion (adding 2 suites at month 12 and 2 additional at month 18) contingent on rolling success metrics (enrollment rate >2 participants/month, median event capture >3 events per participant by month 9), distributing cost and staffing impact across 18 months while maintaining flexibility to halt at 10-bed capacity if metrics deteriorate.

Maintain the 8-bed operational footprint for the full 36-month project, scaling productivity through technician efficiency gains and faster annotation turnaround rather than facility expansion, avoiding €200K capital cost and holding staffing stable, accepting a final cohort size of 40–50 (versus 50–70) and reduced sub-group statistical power for Aim 2.
levers.review: Controls growth ambition vs. operational-risk management and budget certainty. Weakness: The options do not specify how expansion would affect residential property lease negotiations or whether the landlord has contractual flexibility to accommodate 12 suites if the property layout (plumbing, electrical capacity, fire egress) proves inadequate after pilot renovation, creating a hidden constraint on scaling.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences MUST:
     • Chain three SPECIFIC effects: "Immediate: [effect] → Systemic: [impact] → Strategic: [implication]"
     • Include measurable outcomes: "Systemic: [a specific, domain-relevant second-order impact with a measurable indicator, such as a % change, capacity shift, or cost delta]"
     • Explicitly describe trade-offs between core tensions
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional/innovative approach
     • Show clear progression: conservative → moderate → radical
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever` (one field, two sentences, in this order):
     "Controls [Tension A] vs. [Tension B]. Weakness: The options fail to consider [specific factor]."
     Both clauses are mandatory in every response, in this exact order, in one field.
   - For `summary`:
     • Identify ONE critical missing dimension
     • Prescribe CONCRETE addition: "Add '[full strategic option]' to [lever]"

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:", "Conservative:", "Moderate:", "Radical:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences
   - NO "[specific innovative option]" placeholders
   - NO value sets without clear strategic progression

6. **Option Structure Enforcement**
   - Radical option must include emerging tech/business model
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions

Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.
